DMK: Positions the 2026 manifesto as “Dravidian Model 2.0”, claiming this is India’s most successful governance model and promising to take it to “the next level”, but without defining quantitative benchmarks for “2.0”.[web:101][web:135][web:141][web:151]
AIADMK: Vows to restore Jayalalithaa’s “Amma rule”, described as pro‑poor, efficient and people‑centric governance; this is invoked as legacy branding rather than via measurable performance goals.[web:11][web:14][web:139][web:146]
NTK: Articulates a vision of Tamil self‑reliant rule rooted in Tamil language, culture and self‑respect, contrasting it with “Delhi‑controlled” politics; success is framed in emotive, identity terms rather than numeric targets.[web:19][web:22][web:25][web:140][web:144][web:149]
TVK: Projects TVK as a new Tamil party that will protect Tamil identity and language and create a “new political culture” in Tamil Nadu led by Vijay; no objective test is given for when this culture‑change has been achieved.[web:79][web:85][web:90][web:91][web:142][web:147]

DMK: Re‑asserts Dravidian commitments to social justice, secularism and equality – “considering each and every human being as equal” – but offers no explicit targets for lowering inequality, caste‑violence or improving representation beyond current schemes.[web:13][web:32][web:101][web:138][web:151]
AIADMK: Claims AIADMK will stand by the poor, women and marginalised and correct DMK’s alleged anti‑people policies; equality is invoked as moral narrative without new structural equality metrics.[web:11][web:14][web:139]
NTK: Envisions a Tamil society free of caste oppression and built on true social justice for Tamils and marginalised communities, but leaves these as long‑term aspirations without specific indicators of success.[web:22][web:25][web:71][web:140][web:149]
TVK: Promises a Tamil Nadu where women, youth and all communities live with dignity and safety influenced by Thirukkural values, but does not set measurable targets for representation, wage gaps or violence reduction.[web:79][web:85][web:90][web:91][web:147]

DMK: Vows to defend federalism, oppose central overreach and “protect Tamil Nadu’s rights” in Parliament and courts, but does not specify quantifiable outcomes such as particular devolution measures or legal victories as benchmarks.[web:32][web:101][web:135][web:141][web:146]
AIADMK: Pledges to fight both the DMK government and the Union government whenever they act against Tamil Nadu’s interests and to restore the state’s “honour”, but these are not tied to explicit federal reforms or share‑of‑resources metrics.[web:11][web:14][web:139][web:146]
NTK: Frames its project as Tamil self‑rule where policy decisions and resource use are driven primarily by Tamil needs rather than national‑party compulsions; it is presented as a historic correction without clear institutional milestones.[web:19][web:22][web:25][web:140][web:144]
TVK: Declares that TVK will be a strong voice for Tamil Nadu in Delhi, defending Tamil language, education and employment rights, but offers no concrete federal concessions or statutory changes as yardsticks.[web:79][web:90][web:91][web:93]

DMK: Repeats anti‑corruption rhetoric from earlier cycles, promising honest, people‑centric governance, but the 2026 manifesto cycle focuses more on welfare and federalism than on new anti‑graft institutions or data‑transparency laws.[web:32][web:101][web:135][web:136][web:143]
AIADMK: Claims that DMK runs a corrupt, commission‑driven regime and that AIADMK will bring “corruption‑free” government by following Amma’s path, yet does not outline new watchdogs, Lokayukta powers or open‑data commitments.[web:11][web:14][web:139][web:146]
NTK: Presents itself as a clean alternative supportive of the Supreme Court verdict against electoral bonds and calling for transparency, but stops at principles and does not build a fully specified campaign‑finance or anti‑corruption framework.[web:22][web:25][web:140][web:137][web:149]
TVK: Vows a “corruption‑free Tamil Nadu”, an end to dynasty, money and muscle power in politics, and more transparent governance; beyond a general Recruitment Transparency Act, detailed institutional mechanisms (independent ombudsman, public‑data mandates) are not spelled out.[web:79][web:80][web:90][web:91][web:93][web:94][web:142][web:145][web:153]

Policy: Green development, ecology as core value
DMK: Mentions sustainable development and environmental protection under the Dravidian Model 2.0 umbrella, but without explicit environmental KPIs (emissions, air quality, conservation targets) beyond specific schemes.[web:101][web:135][web:151]
AIADMK: Uses generic sustainability phrasing; substantive environmental content in coverage is mostly tied to solar incentives and free power, not to a comprehensive ecological vision.[web:45][web:48][web:139]
NTK: Makes ecology a central ideological pillar – zero‑waste industrial “cycling parks”, strong opposition to ecologically destructive projects, restoration of water bodies – but without quantified targets for pollution reduction or climate resilience.[web:22][web:25][web:71][web:140][web:144][web:152]
TVK: Talks about development that is compatible with environmental protection and safer, cleaner cities, but green commitments remain broad and are not encoded in specific, measurable eco‑goals.[web:79][web:90][web:91][web:147]

DMK: Describes the manifesto as a “superstar” and portrays DMK under Stalin as the only force capable of continuing Tamil Nadu’s progress, but offers limited new institutional mechanisms for youth decision‑making or political‑culture change in itself.[web:13][web:101][web:135][web:141]
AIADMK: Promises to restore stability, protect ordinary citizens and improve law and order compared to DMK’s alleged failures, but does not define institutional reforms or behavioural metrics to assess whether the “political culture” has changed.[web:11][web:14][web:139][web:146]
NTK: Argues that NTK will change governance itself, not just rotate parties in power, by centring Tamil interests and ecology and practising decentralised rule; “different governance” is articulated narratively but without explicit democratic or transparency metrics.[web:22][web:25][web:28][web:140][web:144]
TVK: Explicitly pitches a “new political culture” led by youth, including a Youth Advisory Council and transparent recruitment, but the core claim – that politics will fundamentally change – is inherently non‑falsifiable beyond these partial institutional steps.[web:79][web:80][web:94][web:142][web:147][web:150]
